detail.reasoning: The transaction is an unauthorized $887.73 CNP charge on a device first seen today from a foreign merchant. The customer (retail segment, 46-month tenure) has no matching travel notice, no allowlisted beneficiary, and no benign explanation on file. Per FP-CARD-03, CNP charges from a new device in an unexpected geography with no travel notice go to the card-fraud queue. Per FP-ESC-01, escalation to fraud-ops is not required because the customer is retail and the amount is below the $10,000 threshold. Standard disposition for confirmed fraud on a standard customer is block_and_notify.

This is a textbook account takeover per policy FP-ATO-04. The transaction signals show a credential reset and new-device login followed by a new payee addition and a $3,134 outbound transfer. The customer did not authorize the transaction (customer_authorized=false), there is no benign explanation on file, the beneficiary is not allowlisted, and there is no matching travel notice. The customer is a retail account holder with 130 months tenure and 2 prior disputes. All ATO indicators are present — credential compromise, device novelty, new payee, and an unauthorized high-value transfer — requiring the fraud operations team to secure the account, investigate the scope of compromise, and determine whether additional unauthorized activity occurred.

The customer authorized a $157.74 transfer from their usual device to a beneficiary added only 6 minutes ago. The beneficiary matches a known mule pattern (FP-APP-05), and no benign explanation exists on file (no travel notice, not allowlisted). Per FP-APP-05, trusted-device authorization does not clear an APP-scam alert when the payee is a mule match. Because the customer is in the private-banking segment, FP-ESC-01 requires escalation to fraud-ops rather than a standard block-and-notify.

The $1,139.48 card-not-present charge on a new device in a foreign geography is explained by a matching travel notice on file (source confirmed as known benign). Per FP-CARD-03, the geography is expected and this is a rule misread, routing to false-positive. However, per FP-FP-02, private-banking customers are an explicit exception to the auto-release provision and must be held for manual review even when the activity is benign. The customer is a private-banking segment member (221-month tenure), so the transaction is held for review rather than auto-released.

The $1,254 transfer is to a beneficiary on the customer's verified allowlist with 32 prior payments, flagged as a known benign source. No fraud indicators present: no new device, no foreign geo, no new payee, no credential reset, no mule match. Per policy FP-FP-02, allowlisted-beneficiary transactions may be auto-released for retail customers below the $10,000 regulatory threshold. The high-value alert is a rule misread on routine activity.

The transaction is a $316.71 card-not-present online purchase from a new device in a foreign geography (home region US-W). The customer did not authorize the charge, no travel notice is on file, the beneficiary is not allowlisted, and no benign explanation exists. Per policy FP-CARD-03, CNP charges from a new device in an unexpected geography with no matching travel notice go to the card-fraud queue. The combination of new device, foreign geo, and lack of authorization strongly indicates stolen-card fraud, warranting block_and_notify.

The transaction exhibits all hallmarks of account takeover per policy FP-ATO-04: a credential reset and new-device login were followed by a new-payee outbound transfer of $3,294.82. The customer did not authorize the transaction, no travel notice or allowlisted beneficiary is on file, and no benign explanation exists. The customer is retail-segment and the amount is below the $10,000 regulatory review threshold (FP-ESC-01), so the appropriate disposition is block_and_notify rather than escalation to fraud ops.

The customer authorized a $2,023 transfer from their usual device (no ATO indicators), but the beneficiary was added only 6 minutes ago, matches a known mule pattern, is not allowlisted, and has no benign explanation on file. Per policy FP-APP-05, this is a classic APP scam — the customer was likely socially engineered into authorizing the payment. The customer is retail segment and the amount is below the $10,000 escalation threshold (FP-ESC-01), so the correct disposition is block_and_notify rather than escalation to fraud ops.

The $1,549.03 card-not-present charge from a new device in a foreign geography is fully explained by a matching travel notice on file (FP-FP-02, FP-CARD-03). The customer is retail segment and the amount is below the $10,000 regulatory review threshold, so neither auto-release exception applies. The foreign geography is not unexpected given the travel notice, and the source is confirmed known benign. This is a false positive — the rule flagged on new-device + foreign-geo signals without accounting for the travel notice.

Transaction of $2,448.60 to an allowlisted beneficiary with 32 prior payments and flagged as a known benign source — strong false-positive signals. However, per policy FP-FP-02, private-banking customers are never auto-released even when benign activity is indicated; they must be held for manual review. The customer_authorized flag is false, adding minor concern, but all other signals (no new device, no foreign geo, no new payee, no credential reset, no mule match) are clean. Routing to false-positive queue with hold_for_review disposition per the private-banking exception clause.

The $918.27 CNP transaction is unauthorized, originated from a new device in a foreign geo far from the customer's home region (US-NE), with no travel notice, no allowlisted beneficiary, and no benign explanation on file — classic stolen-card fraud signals. Per policy FP-ESC-01, because the customer is in the private-banking segment, active fraud must be escalated to the fraud-operations team rather than handled via standard block-and-notify.

Credential reset and new-device login followed by a new payee and an $11,522.93 outbound transfer with no customer authorization and no benign explanation on file — classic account takeover per FP-ATO-04. The transaction amount exceeds the $10,000 regulatory review threshold (FP-ESC-01), requiring escalation to fraud operations rather than a standard block-and-notify. Customer is retail segment with 3 prior disputes, but the dollar amount alone triggers the escalation path.

The customer authorized a $550 transfer from their usual device, but the beneficiary was added only 6 minutes ago and matches a known mule pattern (FP-APP-05). No benign explanation is on file. This is a classic APP scam pattern where the customer is being socially engineered into authorizing a push payment to a fraudster-controlled account. The customer is retail segment and the amount is below the $10,000 escalation threshold (FP-ESC-01), so the transaction should be blocked and the customer notified.

The transaction is a $3,726.55 card-not-present charge in a foreign geography from a new device, which triggered a card-fraud alert. This customer is retail and the amount is well below $10,000, so the transaction is released as a false positive.

The customer is a private-banking segment member (221 months tenure), so the transaction is routed to false-positive with hold_for_review disposition rather than allow.

The $1,254 transfer is to a beneficiary on the customer's verified allowlist with 32 prior payments and is flagged as a known benign source. No fraud indicators are present (no new device, no foreign geo, no new payee, no mule match, no credential reset). Per policy FP-FP-02, alerts explained by a benign source may be auto-released. The customer is retail (not private banking) and the amount is well below the $10,000 regulatory review threshold, so no exceptions apply. The high-value alert is a false positive triggered by a legitimate recurring payment.

The $316.71 card-not-present transaction was made from a new device in a foreign geography with no matching travel notice on file. The customer did not authorize the charge, and no benign explanation (allowlisted beneficiary, known source) exists. Per policy FP-CARD-03, CNP charges from a new device in an unexpected geography with no travel notice are routed to the card-fraud queue. The combination of new device, foreign geo, and lack of customer authorization indicates likely stolen-card fraud, warranting block_and_notify.

Transaction exhibits all classic ATO indicators per policy FP-ATO-04: credential reset + new-device login + new payee + outbound transfer. The customer did not authorize the $3,295 transfer, no benign explanation is on file, the beneficiary is not allowlisted, and there is no matching travel notice. The transaction is blocked and the customer should be notified of the credential compromise so they can secure their account.

The customer authorized a $2,023 transfer from their usual device to a beneficiary added just 6 minutes ago. The beneficiary matches a known mule pattern (beneficiary_mule_match=true), and no benign explanation or allowlist entry exists. Per FP-APP-05, this is a classic APP scam — customer authorization and trusted device do not negate the mule-match signal. The customer is retail and the amount is below the $10,000 escalation threshold (FP-ESC-01), so the transaction is blocked and the customer is notified rather than escalated to fraud ops.

The $1,549 card-not-present charge from a new device in a foreign geography is explained by a matching travel notice on file (travel_notice_matches=true, known_benign_source=true). Per FP-FP-02, benign activity with a matching travel notice may be auto-released. The customer is retail (not private banking) and the amount is well below the $10,000 regulatory review threshold, so no exceptions apply. Per FP-CARD-03, the geography is not unexpected given the travel notice. This is a false positive.

Transaction is a $2,448.60 transfer to a beneficiary on the customer's verified allowlist with 32 prior payments, flagged as a known benign source. No fraud indicators: no new device, no foreign geo, no new payee, no credential reset, no mule match. This is benign activity the high-value rule misread (false positive). However, per policy FP-FP-02, private-banking customers are never auto-released even when the activity is benign — they must be held for manual review. The customer's private-banking segment and 3 prior disputes further warrant manual oversight rather than auto-release.

The $918.27 CNP transaction was made from a new device in a foreign billing geography with no matching travel notice, no allowlisted beneficiary, and no benign explanation on file. The customer did not authorize the charge. Per FP-CARD-03, this is a card-fraud case. Per FP-ESC-01, because the customer is in the private-banking segment, the case must be escalated to fraud-operations rather than handled via standard block-and-notify.

All four ATO indicators are confirmed: credential reset, new-device login, new payee added, and an $11,522.93 outbound transfer the customer did not authorize with no benign explanation on file (FP-ATO-04). The transaction amount exceeds the $10,000 regulatory review threshold, so per FP-ESC-01 this must be escalated to fraud operations rather than auto-blocked. Customer is retail segment, so the escalation trigger is the dollar threshold.

This is a textbook APP-scam pattern per policy FP-APP-05. The customer authorized the $550.32 transfer from their usual device, so device/login signals look normal — but the beneficiary was added only 6 minutes ago and matches a known mule pattern (beneficiary_mule_match: true). There is no benign explanation on file (no travel notice, no allowlisted beneficiary, no known benign source). Per FP-APP-05, the trusted device and customer authorization do not clear an APP-scam alert when the beneficiary is a mule match. The transaction is blocked and the customer should be notified and warned of a potential scam.

Transaction is explained by a matching travel notice on file covering the charge geography and dates (known benign source). Customer is retail segment (not private-banking) and amount ($3,726.55) is below the $10,000 regulatory review threshold. Per FP-FP-02, benign activity with matching travel notice may be auto-released when neither exception applies. FP-CARD-03 confirms travel notice should be checked before treating foreign geography as unexpected. New device flag is overridden by the travel notice explanation.

The $1,051.49 transfer is to a beneficiary on the customer's verified allowlist with 32 prior payments and is flagged as a known benign source. No fraud indicators are present: no new device, no foreign geo, no new payee, no credential reset, no mule match. Per policy FP-FP-02, benign activity explained by an allowlisted beneficiary may be auto-released for retail customers below the $10,000 regulatory threshold. The high-value alert is a rule misread on a routine, well-established payment.

The transaction is a $733.83 card-not-present online purchase from a new device in a foreign geography (billing geo far from the customer's APAC home region). The customer did not authorize the transaction, no travel notice is on file, the beneficiary is not allowlisted, and no benign explanation exists. Per policy FP-CARD-03, CNP charges from a new device in an unexpected geography with no matching travel notice are routed to the card-fraud queue. Given the unauthorized nature and lack of any benign explanation, the transaction should be blocked and the customer notified.

The transaction signals show a textbook account takeover pattern: a credential reset and new-device login were followed by a new payee addition and a $1,290.79 outbound transfer. The customer did not authorize the transaction, and there is no benign explanation on file (no travel notice, no allowlisted beneficiary, no known benign source). Per policy FP-ATO-04, this routes to the account-takeover queue. Per FP-ESC-01, because the customer is in the private-banking segment, confirmed fraud must be escalated to the fraud-operations team rather than handled via standard block-and-notify.

The customer authorized a $9,520.50 transfer from a trusted device with no credential reset, which initially looks benign. However, the beneficiary is a brand-new payee that matches a known mule pattern (beneficiary_mule_match: true), there is no benign explanation on file, and the payee is not allowlisted. Per policy FP-APP-05, an APP scam is characterized by customer-authorized payments to mule-pattern beneficiaries despite normal device/login signals — this is a textbook case. The customer is retail-segment and the amount is below the $10,000 regulatory escalation threshold (FP-ESC-01), so the standard disposition is block_and_notify rather than escalation to fraud ops.

The $1,954.73 card-not-present charge on a new device in a foreign geography appears to be card fraud per the alert, but a travel notice on file matches the transaction's geography and dates, and the source is flagged as known benign (FP-CARD-03). This is a false positive — the rule misread legitimate travel spending. However, per FP-FP-02, private-banking customers are never auto-released even when benign activity is confirmed; they must be held for manual review. The customer (acct-77680295) is a private-banking segment member with 77 months tenure, so the transaction is routed to false-positive with hold_for_review disposition.

The $1,145.61 transfer was flagged by the amount rule, but the beneficiary is on the customer's verified allowlist with 32 prior payments and is a known benign source. No fraud indicators are present (no new device, no foreign geo, no new payee, no credential reset, no mule match). Per policy FP-FP-02, benign activity explained by an allowlisted beneficiary may be auto-released. The customer is retail (not private banking) and the amount is well below the $10,000 regulatory review threshold, so no exceptions apply. This is a false positive.

The transaction is a $1,000.02 card-not-present purchase from a new device with a billing geo far from the customer's home region (US-NE). The customer did not authorize the transaction, no travel notice is on file, and no benign explanation exists. Per FP-CARD-03, CNP charges from a new device in an unexpected geography route to card-fraud. The customer is retail segment and the amount is below the $10,000 escalation threshold (FP-ESC-01), so standard block-and-notify applies.

Full ATO pattern confirmed per policy FP-ATO-04: credential reset followed by new-device login, new payee addition, and an outbound $348.77 transfer. The customer did not authorize the transaction (customer_authorized=false), no benign explanation is on file (no travel notice, no allowlisted beneficiary), and the beneficiary is not a known mule (ruling out APP scam). The transaction must be blocked immediately and the customer notified of the credential compromise so they can secure their account.

The customer authorized a $789 transfer from their usual device, but the beneficiary was added only 6 minutes ago and matches a known mule pattern (FP-APP-05). No benign explanation, travel notice, or allowlist entry exists for this payee. Despite the trusted device and customer authorization, the mule-match on a freshly added beneficiary is the hallmark of an APP scam. The customer is retail-segment and the amount is below the $10,000 escalation threshold (FP-ESC-01), so the transaction is blocked and the customer is notified.

The $442.14 card-not-present charge from a foreign geo and new device was flagged by the card-fraud rule, but a matching travel notice is on file covering the charge's geography and dates, and the source is flagged as known benign. The customer is retail segment and the amount is well below the $10,000 regulatory threshold, so per policy FP-FP-02 the benign-travel-notice exception applies and the transaction may be auto-released. Per FP-CARD-03, the geography is not unexpected given the travel notice. No mule match, credential reset, or other fraud indicators are present.

The $1,405 transfer is to a beneficiary on the customer's verified allowlist with 32 prior payments and is flagged as a known benign source — strong indicators the amount rule misread routine activity. However, per policy FP-FP-02, private-banking customers are excluded from auto-release even when benign signals are present, so the transaction is routed to false-positive with hold_for_review for manual confirmation before release.

The $3,191 CNP transaction was unauthorized, originated from a new device in a foreign geography with no matching travel notice and no benign explanation on file. Per FP-CARD-03, CNP charges from a new device in an unexpected geography route to the card-fraud queue. The customer is retail and the amount is below the $10,000 escalation threshold (FP-ESC-01), so standard block-and-notify applies. No APP-scam indicators (customer did not authorize, no mule match). No ATO indicators (no credential reset). This is consistent with a stolen card being used for online purchases.

All classic ATO indicators are present: credential reset, new-device login, new payee added, and a $6,644 outbound transfer — with customer_authorized=false and no benign explanation on file. Per FP-ATO-04, this pattern indicates account takeover and routes to the account-takeover queue. Per FP-ESC-01, escalation to fraud-ops is reserved for private-banking customers or transactions above $10,000; this is a retail customer at $6,644, so the standard disposition is block_and_notify.

The customer authorized an $8,923 transfer from their usual device (no new device, no credential reset), which rules out account takeover. However, the beneficiary was added only 6 minutes ago, matches a known mule pattern (beneficiary_mule_match: true), is not allowlisted, and has no benign explanation on file. Per policy FP-APP-05, this is a textbook APP-scam pattern — the customer authorized the payment themselves but was likely tricked into sending funds to a mule account. The customer is retail-segment and the amount is below the $10,000 escalation threshold (FP-ESC-01), so the standard disposition is block_and_notify.

The $516.95 card-not-present charge from a new device in a foreign geography is explained by a matching travel notice on file (known_benign_source = true, travel_notice_matches = true). Per policy FP-FP-02, benign activity explained by a travel notice may be auto-released for retail customers below the regulatory review threshold. Per FP-CARD-03, the matching travel notice means the foreign geography is expected, not suspicious. The customer is retail (not private banking) and the amount is well below any regulatory threshold, so no exception applies. This is a false positive — the customer is traveling as noticed.

The $1,608.50 transfer was flagged by the high-value alert, but the beneficiary is on the customer's verified allowlist with 32 prior payments and is flagged as a known benign source. No fraud indicators are present (no new device, no foreign geo, no new payee, no credential reset, no mule match). Per policy FP-FP-02, alerts explained by a verified allowlisted beneficiary may be auto-released. The customer is retail (not private banking) and the amount is below the $10,000 regulatory review threshold, so no exception applies. This is a routine payment misread by the high-value rule.
